channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Will Netanyahu Derail the Iran War Cease-Fire?
item.description: Israel’s offensive in Lebanon is threatening the tenuous truce.

item.title: Will ASEAN Welcome Myanmar Back Into Its Fold?
item.description: The official appointment of coup leader Min Aung Hlaing as president is likely to hasten a shift within the bloc.

item.title: Rutte Defends NATO’s Iran War Response Amid Trump’s Ire
item.description: The NATO chief spoke the day after a tense White House meeting with the U.S. president.

item.title: A Wait in the Strait
item.description: Shipping companies are reacting with caution as Hormuz cease-fire terms remain uncertain.

item.title: Even Hungary’s skewed elections might not save Viktor Orban
item.description: The ruling party is playing dirty. But our analysis shows the liberal opposition has a real chance of winning

item.title: Trump’s Recipe for Accelerated U.S. Decline
item.description: Erratic militarism combined with increased defense spending would only bring long-term decay.

item.title: The Man Who Shaped Washington’s View of the Middle East
item.description: Brett McGurk advised four presidents on a contested region—but to what end?

item.title: America’s War Is Adding to Africa’s Debt Burden
item.description: The US-Israeli war against Iran has shown, once again, that when American policy decisions push Treasury yields higher, African sovereigns pay the price. African governments must ensure that this is the last geopolitical crisis that drives up their borrowing costs through no fault of their own.

item.title: The Iran Cease-Fire Has Only Divided the War
item.description: Instead of ending the fighting, the current deal has mostly managed to complicate it.

item.title: How Ukraine Benefited from Trump’s Iran War
item.description: Countries are lining up for military deals with Kyiv.

item.title: The Decline and Fall of the Dollar Empire
item.description: The United States does not have an emperor like ancient Rome's Nero, but its politics are increasingly subject to rule by one man who threatens its democratic traditions. And just as Nero undermined the Roman denarius—arguably the world's first international currency—America's ruler poses a growing threat to his country's currency.

item.title: This Isn’t a 1970s Oil Shock
item.description: The Iran war has disrupted a different, more fragile global energy system.

item.title: Decorated Soldier Charged in Australia War Crimes Case
item.description: The controversy over Roberts-Smith being charged for his alleged actions is rooted in a politically polarized failure to understand the importance of rules of engagement in conflict zones.

item.title: That ugly ballroom epitomises the story of Donald Trump’s presidency
item.description: It illustrates his key means, methods, and goals, and reveals the true nature of his achievements

item.title: An environmentalist, a landowner and a libertarian walk into a barn
item.description: How a pipeline tangled political alliances in Iowa

item.title: Donald Trump’s ceasefire shows how America has changed
item.description: Contrast it with George Bush senior’s victory in Kuwait

item.title: Will California’s next governor be a fighter or a fixer?
item.description: Candidates with buzzy national profiles are leading those with local records

item.title: The pros and cons of stretch goals
item.description: Setting really tough targets encourages risk-taking, for good and bad

item.title: Japan’s mighty carmakers are in serious trouble
item.description: They will need bold thinking to survive

item.title: A giant succession wave is coming for family businesses
item.description: Many will not survive

item.title: There is little prospect of legalising abortion in Brazil
item.description: Other large countries in Latin America have either legalised or decriminalised

item.title: Sir Keir Starmer is Britain’s best hope for legalising assisted dying
item.description: He should stop dawdling

item.title: Recriminations over Iran have heightened the risk of a break-up of NATO
item.description: Europe must prepare to defend itself

item.title: Cambodia honours a life-saving rat
item.description: The rodent helped clear more than 100 unexploded mines

item.title: China may be building a big new airbase in the South China Sea
item.description: But analysts are scratching their heads over the timing and location

item.title: AI-generated micro-dramas are shaking up entertainment in China
item.description: Regulators threaten to spoil the fun

item.title: The West is doing more to combat China’s covert activity abroad
item.description: But China is paying little attention

item.title: Hospitals never recovered from covid-19
item.description: They are stuck in a deadly doom loop

item.title: Business
The weekly cartoon
Politics
Earth and Moon, then and now
Sir Demis Hassabis wants to automate drug design
item.description: We speak to the boss of Google DeepMind

item.title: Why a big country like Italy acts as if it were small
item.description: Equivocating between Trump and Europe suits a history of switching sides

item.title: Viktor Orban is bashing Ukraine for votes
item.description: Hungary’s election poisons relations between Hungary and its neighbour

item.title: Europe’s joint nuclear-fusion project needs Russian expertise
item.description: At ITER Russian and Western scientists still work together—slowly

item.title: Most Syrians in Germany are there to stay
item.description: Sending back masses of immigrants would be foolish and impossible

item.title: A deadly conflict in Malawi raises questions about conservation
item.description: How can humans coexist with wild animals?

item.title: Burkina Faso’s government is committing war crimes
item.description: While fighting jihadism, it has killed more civilians than the jihadists themselves

item.title: How anarchist was Africa?
item.description: Precolonial statelessness may have been a sign of success, not failure

item.title: Recent revolts in Africa reflect widespread anger with the status quo
item.description: But can they bring lasting change?

item.title: Recent revolts in Africa reflect anger with the status quo
item.description: But can they bring lasting change?

item.title: Africa’s protests span countries, classes and causes
item.description: But can they bring lasting change?

item.title: Trump’s Next Coup Attempt
item.description: While turning a foreign war into a domestic dictatorship is complicated and difficult, Donald Trump could try to do so in one of five ways. But even the most likely scenario—using an act of terror as a pretext to delay or discredit the midterms—will not work if Americans are vigilant and refuse to obey in advance.

item.title: New Zealand’s Climate Plan Challenged in High Court
item.description: At the heart of the judicial review is the government’s removal of dozens of climate policies that had formed part of New Zealand’s first emissions reduction plan.

item.title: Some thoughts on prohibition
item.description: Our report from 1923

item.title: Iran’s Strategic Victory
item.description: The US-Israeli war will be remembered as yet another episode of powerful countries falling into the trap of asymmetric warfare, with the ceasefire ratifying what any competent military planner should have anticipated. But while the US might be able to absorb the shock of another defeat, Israel is no superpower.

item.title: India’s religious minorities face harsher anti-conversion laws
item.description: Exhumations fuel communal tensions

item.title: How Pakistan emerged as an unlikely broker of peace in the Gulf
item.description: The troubled country has cannily managed its ties with the warring parties

item.title: The Problem of Assessing Democratic Decline
item.description: Democracy is in retreat across much of the world, erasing nearly five decades of hard-won gains. Understanding this trend and how to reverse it requires better ways to evaluate democratic governance and an honest reckoning with how major powers undermine rights and norms beyond their own borders.

item.title: How Bad Will the Economy Be?
item.description: Economic performance in the United States has proved unexpectedly resilient in recent years, withstanding even Donald Trump’s return to the White House. But, from a failed war of choice against Iran to reckless deregulation, fundamental risks are piling up, and uncertainty has become so extreme that economic forecasters cannot even assign probabilities to them. While that rules out confident prediction, the global connectedness of these risks means that what comes next is certain to reverberate worldwide.

item.title: The Big Picture
America’s war on Iran has changed the Middle East—for the worse
item.description: This week’s ceasefire leaves the region less secure than when the war began

item.title: Stakes Rise for Pakistan as it Prepares to Host US-Iran Face-to-Face Talks
item.description: If talks progress and the war ends with a permanent solution, Pakistan will emerge as a crucial player in West Asia. If not, it can expect turmoil.

item.title: One neat trick to end extreme poverty
item.description: An age-old problem might have a surprisingly simple solution

item.title: Can the secondary market allay private-credit fears?
item.description: In the short run, a bit. In the long run, a lot more

item.title: The latest Italian banking whodunnit has it all
item.description: A a coup, a conspiracy and even a (possibly unrelated) corpse

item.title: South Korea’s AI industrial policy meets the energy shock
item.description: The collision will not be pretty

item.title: China’s Economic Sanctions Against Japan: An Assessment
item.description: Beijing makes a strategy shift.

item.title: Donald Trump is the war’s biggest loser
item.description: There is a reason he wants an exit from Iran

item.title: How war has made a 33-year-old the Czech Republic’s richest man
item.description: The rapid rise of CSG, Europe’s newest defence giant

item.title: A ceasefire will not stop the Iran war’s economic consequences
item.description: Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens, expect lasting change to energy markets

item.title: A ceasefire will not prevent the Iran war’s economic harm
item.description: Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens, expect lasting change to energy markets

item.title: The Iran War, ASEAN and Global Supply Chains
item.description: A logistics specialist discusses the nuts and bolts of getting goods to market as the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran continues into its second month.

item.title: The Gas Inside Your AI Chip
item.description: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has cut off a key source of helium, threatening the global semiconductor supply chain.

item.title: America’s Pro-War Elites Must Be Held Accountable
item.description: Advocates for the disastrous adventure in Iran shouldn’t escape responsibility.

item.title: Southeast Asian Economies’ Growth to Slow in 2026, World Bank Says
item.description: The disruption of oil and gas supplies from the Middle East is compounding the impacts of the Trump administration's tariffs.

item.title: India Upgrades Military Infrastructure in the Northeast on War Footing
item.description: External threats have prompted India’s enhanced military presence and upgradation of infrastructure along the Siliguri Corridor and in the Northeast.

item.title: A Last Chance for Hungary
item.description: Orban’s mafia state could fall—or cement itself.

item.title: How a Cease-Fire Can Lead to Disaster
item.description: The first Gulf War’s lessons for what to do—and not do—in Iran.

item.title: Vietnam’s Top Leader to Visit China Next Week, Report Says
item.description: If confirmed, the trip to Beijing would be To Lam's first since his historic appointment as state president earlier this week.

item.title: American Chamber of Commerce Celebrates 15th Anniversary in Mongolia
item.description: Since its founding, AmCham has played an important role in shaping U.S.-Mongolia business relations.

item.title: Serbia Hedges Its Bets With Chinese High-Speed Missiles
item.description: For Belgrade, the logic behind the acquisition of Chinese CM-400AKG missiles is multifaceted.

item.title: Can Uchral Hold On? Beyond Mongolia’s Leadership Shake-Up
item.description: The young leader's sudden rise raises questions about the future of Mongolia's democracy and its balanced approach to foreign policy.

item.title: Japan and Australia Deepen Defense Ties Amid Middle East Turmoil and North Korea Missile Test
item.description: In an era defined by overlapping crises and intensifying strategic competition, the two nation's security partnership is more important than ever.

item.title: Over 2 Consecutive Days, North Korea Fires Ballistic Missiles Toward East Sea
item.description: Whatever goodwill Lee’s expression of regret over past drone incursions may have produced, Pyongyang's hostile posture toward South Korea remains unchanged.

item.title: NSW’s Coal Reforms are Ambitious, but Diplomatic Engagement is Needed
item.description: The state has shown that domestic change is possible at the sub-national level. Can Australia leverage that momentum to shape the sector’s regional transition?

item.title: Kazakhstan’s New Constitution: Reform or Power Consolidation?
item.description: Authoritarian systems rarely resist reform. Instead, they use it as a means of renewing and strengthening themselves.
